summary: McKenzie describes a frustrating CSS rendering bug in Internet Explorer caused by using a standards-compliant DOCTYPE declaration. Despite following web standards best practices, IE ignored his CSS margin:auto centering. The fix requires changing the DOCTYPE to XHTML, which in turn demands updating all self-closing tags to pass validation. It's a brief vent about the cascading annoyances of cross-browser compatibility in the mid-2000s web.
key_points: Using a standards-compliant HTML 4.01 Transitional DOCTYPE caused IE to ignore CSS margin:auto for centering
Fixing the issue requires switching to an XHTML DOCTYPE, which creates additional markup changes
Being a 'good web citizen' with standards compliance paradoxically broke the page in the dominant browser
money_quotes: It seems that when I was a good web citizen and made all my pages nice and standards compliant via liberal use of DOCTYPE I apparently instructed IE to please ignore my CSS settings (come again?!)
key_insight: In the mid-2000s web, following standards compliance could paradoxically break your site in Internet Explorer, forcing developers into cascading workarounds.
